FenceStatus.title: FenceStatus
FenceStatus.description: Represents the status of the electric fence.

id.description: Unique identifier for the FenceStatus entity.

status.description: The current status of the fence (LEGAL or ILLEGAL).

lastUpdated.description: Timestamp of the last status update.

pulseValue.description: The pulse rate of the fence (e.g., '1.2 pulses/sec').

SensorData.title: SensorData
SensorData.description: Represents sensor data from the IoT device.

id.description: Unique identifier for the SensorData entity.

temperature.description: The temperature reading from the sensor in Celsius.

gasValue.description: The gas level reading from the MQ sensor (e.g., in PPM).

lastRead.description: Timestamp of when sensor data was last read.

SensorRequest.title: SensorRequest
SensorRequest.description: A request to the IoT device to read and update sensor data.

requestedAt.description: Timestamp of when the sensor data refresh was requested.

definition.description: Stores the status of the electric fence.  Since this application is using Realtime Database and not Firestore, this path is conceptual. `fenceStatusId` is a unique ID.

params.name: fenceStatusId
params.description: Unique identifier for the fence status document.

definition.description: Stores sensor data for the fence. Since this application is using Realtime Database and not Firestore, this path is conceptual. `sensorDataId` is a unique ID.

params.name: sensorDataId
params.description: Unique identifier for the sensor data document.

definition.description: Stores requests for the IoT device to perform a sensor read.

params.name: requestId
params.description: Unique identifier for the request document.

firestore.reasoning: Given the error reported, the application is using the Firebase Realtime Database and not Firestore. The error indicates that the Firebase app was initialized without a project ID, leading to the failure of retrieving the database URL. This problem is not related to Firestore structure but to Firebase initialization. There is no need for a complex Firestore structure, as the application only uses Firebase to sync a status (LEGAL/ILLEGAL) between the admin panel and the user dashboard, and some static sensor data. This can be achieved with a very simple Realtime Database structure. The app only stores FenceStatus and SensorData. These entities are simple and do not require complex authorization rules. A straightforward approach is to use a simple key-value pair structure in Realtime Database. This approach directly addresses the application requirements with minimal overhead. Since this is Realtime Database, the recommendation does not require any rules or authorization independence, as those concepts are inherent in Firestore and its rule structure. The requested changes do not need to change to Firestore.
